content:
In the previous article, I discussed a common but dangerous oversimplification:

Async work should not be reduced to Promise.resolve() followed by setState().

The real problem is not that setState() is inherently wrong. The problem is that we often treat every asynchronous result as if it were the same kind of thing.

API request completed → write to state
Form submitted successfully → write to state
WebSocket message received → write to state
Background job updated → write to state

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

From the UI's perspective, all of these eventually cause something on the screen to update. But from the perspective of data flow semantics, they are not the same kind of work.

Some async work reads data.

Some async work changes the state of a remote or local system.

Some async work continuously observes changes over time.

In other words, they should at least be separated into three different concepts:

• Query

• Mutation

• Stream

Many frontend architectures eventually flatten all of them back into the same pipeline:

async work resolved → update state → render again

This path is familiar and convenient. It is also one of the major sources of state confusion. Because the difference between Query, Mutation, and Stream is not merely their API shape. They have different requirements around data ownership, lifecycle, caching, invalidation, cancellation, retries, and consistency.

Query: Reading a Data Fact

A Query is often understood as something like this:

const users = await fetchUsers()

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

But this is only the execution form. It is not the semantic meaning.

The essence of a Query is:

I want to know the current state of a particular data fact.

For example:

GET /users
GET /users/:id
GET /posts?authorId=1

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

These operations mean that we are retrieving a piece of identifiable, cacheable, and revalidatable data. That is why one of the most important properties of a Query is identity: how this piece of data is identified within the system.

For example:

users.list()
users.detail(id)
posts.byAuthor(authorId)

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Once a Query has identity, it should not merely be treated as temporary async logic inside a Component.

The same Query result may be consumed by multiple places:

• a list page needs users

• a detail page needs user

• a search box may need partial user data

• permission checks may depend on user data

• background synchronization may need to revalidate users

If every place performs its own fetch → setState, data ownership becomes confused. Eventually, you end up with multiple pieces of state that look like the same data, but have no semantic relationship with one another.

A Query should not belong to the render lifecycle. It should be a Resource Node inside the Reactive Graph. It owns its identity, knows when it should reload, knows which sources it depends on, and manages its own loading and stale states.

The responsibility of the Render Layer is simply to subscribe to and read the Snapshot of this Node.

Mutation: Changing System State

A Mutation often looks like this:

await updateUser(id, patch)
setUser(updatedUser)

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

The real complexity of a Mutation is not sending the request.

The hard part is what happens after the request succeeds:

Which data in the system is no longer trustworthy?

For example:

PATCH /users/:id

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

This Mutation may affect:

GET /users/:id
GET /users
GET /teams/:teamId/members
GET /audit-logs
GET /permissions

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

If we only treat Mutation as “sending a request,” then after success, we usually end up with patterns like:

setUser(updatedUser)
refetchUsers()
refetchUserDetail()
invalidateQueries(["users"])

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

These approaches can work. The problem is that the semantics are often scattered across the application. Component A knows it should refetch the list. Component B knows it should update the detail. Some hook knows it should invalidate the cache. Some effect adds an extra synchronization step.

Over time, the system enters a difficult-to-maintain state:

The impact of a Mutation is global, but the logic for handling its consequences is local.

This is the biggest difference between Mutation and Query.

The core question of a Query is:

Which data do I want to read?

The core question of a Mutation is:

What did I change, and which existing data became invalid because of it?

A Mutation should not merely return data. It should also declare its impact on the data graph.

Semantically, it should be closer to this:

updateUser.mutate({
id,
patch,
invalidates: [
users.detail(id),
users.list(),
teamMembers.byUser(id),
],
})

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Or:

mutation(updateUser, {
invalidates: ({ id }) => [
userResource(id),
usersListResource(),
],
})

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

The exact API shape is not the important part.

The important part is the semantic model:

A Mutation should describe the invalidation relationships it creates, instead of pushing invalidation responsibility into the UI.

If a successful Mutation requires certain Queries to be revalidated, that should be the responsibility of the data layer, not a side effect inside some Component.

A Component is not the owner of the data graph. A Component only knows what it wants to display. It should not be responsible for understanding which nodes in the entire data world need to be recalculated after a Mutation changes something.

The better abstraction is:

Mutation changes the data graph
↓
related nodes become invalid
↓
Reactive Graph propagates the change
↓
UI updates passively

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Stream: Not a Continuous Query, but a Long-Lived Data Source

Streams deal with things like WebSocket, Server-Sent Events, AI token streaming, or real-time collaborative updates.

Many times, we simplify Stream handling into this:

socket.onmessage = event => {
setMessages(prev => [...prev, event.data])
}

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Or:

for await (const chunk of stream) {
setText(prev => prev + chunk)
}

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

This is fine for small demos, but it quickly exposes another semantic mismatch.

A Stream is a long-lived data relationship.

A Query has a beginning and an end.

A Mutation has submission and completion.

A Stream continuously pushes data over time.

Its concerns are not limited to loading, success, and error.

It also needs to deal with:

• connection health and reconnection

• the distinction between Current Value and Stable Value

• backpressure

• subscription lifecycle

• whether multiple consumers should share the same Stream

These concerns cannot be properly described by a normal Promise<T>.

For example, consider AI token streaming:

for await (const token of response) {
setText(prev => prev + token)
}

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

On the surface, this is just appending text.

But semantically, there are at least three different states:

currentValue // the value currently flowing
stableValue // the completed value that can be treated as stable
streamStatus // streaming / completed / cancelled / error

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

If we use a single state value to represent all of these, they are easily mixed together. The UI may need to display tokens in real time. The cache may only want to store the completed result. Derived State may not want to recompute on every token. Effects may only need to run after the stream is completed.

This is the biggest difference between Stream and Query. A Query result can be treated as a snapshot. A Stream result is a trajectory of changes over time. It should be modeled as a long-lived Async Resource, not as an endless setState loop inside a Component.

When All Three Are Mixed Together, the System Loses Its Boundaries

If Query, Mutation, and Stream are all flattened into the same process:

async work → setState → render

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

the system gradually loses its Async Boundary.

You eventually start asking questions like:

• Is this loading state for a Query, or is it a Mutation pending state?

• Is this error caused by data fetching, or by submission failure?

• Is this data a stable result, or an intermediate Stream value?

• Who triggered this refetch?

• Is this effect synchronizing Derived State, or patching up invalidation after a Mutation?

• Is this state the source of truth, or just a snapshot of an async result?

• Why does this Component know so many data invalidation rules?

These questions are not obvious at first. In small applications, the differences between Query, Mutation, and Stream can still be remembered by humans. But as the system grows, relying on human memory to maintain semantic boundaries eventually fails.

What remains is a pile of code that looks reasonable, but is semantically confused:

useEffect(() => {
refetch()
}, [submitted])
useEffect(() => {
if (success) {
setLocalUser(data)
}
}, [success, data])
useEffect(() => {
if (streamDone) {
saveToCache(text)
}
}, [streamDone])
useEffect(() => {
if (mutationSuccess) {
reloadList()
reloadDetail()
reloadPermissions()
}
}, [mutationSuccess])

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

These examples are not necessarily wrong. But they reveal something important:

The async semantics of the system are hidden inside the UI lifecycle.

Once async semantics are hidden inside Components, it becomes difficult to understand where the real ownership of the data flow lives.

Render Should Not Be Responsible for Understanding Async Semantics

The responsibility of Render should be simple:

Describe what the UI looks like under the current Snapshot.

But the responsibility of Query, Mutation, and Stream is different:

Describe how async data exists, changes, becomes invalid, stabilizes, and is consumed.

These two responsibilities should not be mixed.

When Render needs to know too many details about async events, the architectural boundary has already started to shift in the wrong direction.

For example, a Component may look like this:

const users = useUsersQuery()
const updateUser = useUpdateUserMutation()
const stream = useUserActivityStream()
useEffect(() => {
if (updateUser.success) {
users.refetch()
}
}, [updateUser.success])
useEffect(() => {
if (stream.completed) {
users.refetch()
}
}, [stream.completed])

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

This kind of code is very common in frontend applications.

But from the perspective of ownership, it reveals something deeper:

The Component is patching holes in the data graph.

It is no longer just rendering. It knows which Queries should be updated after a Mutation succeeds. It knows which data should be refreshed after a Stream completes. It knows causal relationships between async states.

Once this knowledge is scattered across Components, the system becomes increasingly difficult to reason about. The rules of data flow are no longer centralized in the data layer. They are hidden across hooks, effects, callbacks, and lifecycles.

A Better Model: Return Async Work to the Reactive Graph

If we model Query, Mutation, and Stream as different kinds of nodes in the Reactive Graph, the responsibilities become much clearer:

Query (resource) → readable, cacheable, invalidatable data node
Mutation (mutation) → operation node that changes system state and declares invalidation relationships
Stream (streamResource) → long-lived node for time-series data, with current value and stable value separated

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Under this model, the UI only consumes Snapshots:

const userView = useResource(user)
const streamView = useStreamResource(chatStream)

Enter fullscr
